intro:
Justification is the gracious judicial act by which God declares a believing sinner righteous in his sight. It is a forensic (legal) verdict — the opposite of condemnation — in which God pronounces the ungodly "not guilty" and "righteous," not by making them inwardly perfect (that is sanctification), but by crediting Christ’s righteousness to their account. It is a once-for-all declaration of status, not a process.

Its necessity lies in universal guilt: "all have sinned" (Romans 3:23), and "by works of the law no human being will be justified" (Romans 3:20; Galatians 2:16). Its ground is the work of Christ — God presented him as a propitiation so that he might be "just and the justifier of the one who has faith in Jesus" (Romans 3:24–26); the sinless one was "made… to be sin" for us "so that in him we might become the righteousness of God" (2 Corinthians 5:21).

Its instrument is faith alone: "we hold that one is justified by faith apart from works of the law" (Romans 3:28), so that "since we have been justified by faith, we have peace with God" (Romans 5:1). Abraham is the proof: he "believed God, and it was counted to him as righteousness" — a gift reckoned, not wages earned (Romans 4:4–5; Genesis 15:6). The righteousness received is Christ’s, "not… a righteousness of my own that comes from the law" (Philippians 3:9).

James’s statement that a person is "justified by works and not by faith alone" (James 2:24) uses the word in its sense of being shown or vindicated as righteous — genuine faith proves itself in works — and so complements rather than contradicts Paul. The fruit of justification is profound: "there is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus" (Romans 8:1), for it is God who justifies (Romans 8:33–34). It is the heart of the gospel.
